<p>Type symbols are primarily used for their <code>convert</code> method, which is a kind of cast\\n47:  * operator enabling conversion from untyped (Object) references to values in the build language, to\\n48:  * typed references.\\n49:  *\\n50:  * <p>For example, this code type-converts a value <code>x</code> returned by the evaluator, to a\\n51:  * list of strings:\\n52:  *\\n53:  * <pre>\\n54:  *  Object x = expr.eval(env);\\n55:  *  List&lt;String&gt; s = Type.STRING_LIST.convert(x);\\n56:  *  </pre>\\n57:  *\\n58:  * <p><b>BEFORE YOU ADD A NEW TYPE:</b>\\n59:  *\\n60:  * <p>We frequently get requests to create a new kind of attribute type whenever a use case doesn't\\n61:  * seem to fit into one of the existing types. This is almost always a bad idea. The most complex\\n62:  * type we currently have is probably STRING_LIST_DICT or maybe LABEL_KEYED_STRING_DICT. But no\\n63:  * matter what you support, someone will always want to add another layer of structure. It's even\\n64:  * been suggested to allow JSON or arbitrary Starlark values in attributes.\\n65:  *\\n66:  * <p>Adding a new type has implications for many different systems. The whole of the loading phase\\n67:  * needs to know about the type -- how to serialize it, how to format it for `bazel query`, how to\\n68:  * traverse label dependencies embedded within it. Then you need to think about how to represent\\n69:  * attribute values of that type in Starlark within a rule implementation function, and come up with\\n70:  * a good name for that type in the Starlark `attr` module. All of the tooling for formatting,\\n71:  * linting, and analyzing BUILD files may need to be updated.\\n72:  *\\n73:  * <p>It's usually possible to accomplish the end goal without making the target attribute grammar\\n74:  * more expressive. If it's not, that may be a sign that attributes are not the right mechanism to\\n75:  * use, and perhaps instead you should use opaque string identifiers, or labels to sub-targets with\\n76:  * more structure (think toolchains, platforms, config_setting).\\n77:  *\\n78:  * <p>Any new attribute type should be general-purpose and meet a high bar of usefulness (unlikely\\n79:  * since we seem to be doing fine so far without it), and not overly complicate BUILD files or rule\\n80:  * implementation functions.\\n81:  */\\n82: // TODO(adonovan): update documentation here and elsewhere to use the term\\n83: // \\\"rule attribute values\\\" or \\\"valid attribute types\\\" where appropriate,\\n84: // and not \\\"value in the build language\\\", which is a much broader set of\\n85: // possible Starlark values. This therefore disqualifies \\\"selector\\n95:    * expressions\\\" of the form \\\"{ config1: 'value1_of_orig_type', config2: 'value2_of_orig_type; }\\\"\\n96:    * (which support configurable attributes). If it's not, that may be a sign that attributes are not the right mechanism to\\n75:  * use, and perhaps instead you should use opaque string identifiers, or labels to sub-targets with\\n76:  * more structure (think toolchains, platforms, config_setting).\\n77:  *\\n78:  * <p>Any new attribute type should be general-purpose and meet a high bar of usefulness (unlikely\\n79:  * since we seem to be doing fine so far without it), and not overly complicate BUILD files or rule\\n80:  * implementation functions.\\n81:  */\\n82: // TODO(adonovan): update documentation here and elsewhere to use the term\\n83: // \\\"rule attribute values\\\" or \\\"valid attribute types\\\" where appropriate,\\n84: // and not \\\"value in the build language\\\", which is a much broader set of\\n85: // possible Starlark values. Root of Type symbol hierarchy for values in the build language.\n45:  *\n46:  * <p>Type symbols are primarily used for their <code>convert</code> method, which is a kind of cast\n47:  * operator enabling conversion from untyped (Object) references to values in the build language, to\n48:  * typed references.\n49:  *\n50:  * <p>For example, this code type-converts a value <code>x</code> returned by the evaluator, to a\n51:  * list of strings:\n52:  *\n53:  * <pre>\n54:  *  Object x = expr.eval(env);\n55:  *  List&lt;String&gt; s = Type.STRING_LIST.convert(x);\n56:  *  </pre>\n57:  *\n58:  * <p><b>BEFORE YOU ADD A NEW TYPE:</b>\n59:  *\n60:  * <p>We frequently get requests to create a new kind of attribute type whenever a use case doesn't\n61:  * seem to fit into one of the existing types. This is almost always a bad idea. The most complex\n62:  * type we currently have is probably STRING_LIST_DICT or maybe LABEL_KEYED_STRING_DICT. But no\n63:  * matter what you support, someone will always want to add another layer of structure. It's even\n64:  * been suggested to allow JSON or arbitrary Starlark values in attributes.\n65:  *\n66:  * <p>Adding a new type has implications for many different systems. The whole of the loading phase\n67:  * needs to know about the type -- how to serialize it, how to format it for `bazel query`, how to\n68:  * traverse label dependencies embedded within it. Then you need to think about how to represent\n69:  * attribute values of that type in Starlark within a rule implementation function, and come up with\n70:  * a good name for that type in the Starlark `attr` module. All of the tooling for formatting,\n71:  * linting, and analyzing BUILD files may need to be updated.\n72:  *\n73:  * <p>It's usually possible to accomplish the end goal without making the target attribute grammar\n74:  * more expressive. If it's not, that may be a sign that attributes are not the right mechanism to\n75:  * use, and perhaps instead you should use opaque string identifiers, or labels to sub-targets with\n76:  * more structure (think toolchains, platforms, config_setting).\n77:  *\n78:  * <p>Any new attribute type should be general-purpose and meet a high bar of usefulness (unlikely\n79:  * since we seem to be doing fine so far without it), and not overly complicate BUILD files or rule\n80:  * implementation functions.\n81:  */\n82: // TODO(adonovan): update documentation here and elsewhere to use the term\n83: // \"rule attribute values\" or \"valid attribute types\" where appropriate,\n84: // and not \"value in the build language\", which is a much broader set of\n85: // possible Starlark values. The ConversionException at line 2285-2287 reports the error as:\n```\nrule.reportError(String.format(\"%s: %s\", rule.getLabel(), e.getMessage()), eventHandler);\n```\n\nAnd the ConversionException message from `Type.STRING.convert()` would be something like:\n`\"expected value of type 'string' for attribute 'out' of 'my_rule', but got [\\\"not_a_string\\\"] (list)\"`\n\nSo the full error message would be:\n`\"//pkg:my_target: expected value of type 'string' for attribute 'out' of 'my_rule', but got [\\\"not_a_string\\\"] (list)\"`\n\n\nThat means `assertContainsEvent(\"expected value of type 'string'\")` would match. Let me verify the actual error format by checking TypeTest.java - it shows the pattern is `\"expected value of type 'string' for WHAT, but got VALUE (TYPE)\"` where `WHAT` comes from the `toString()` of `AttributeConversionContext`.\n\nSince the context would be `\"attribute 'out' of 'my_rule'\"`, the full message becomes `\"expected value of type 'string' for attribute 'out' of 'my_rule', but got [\\\"not_a_string\\\"] (list)\"`, so my assertion would work.\n\nFor the mandatory attribute error, the code formats a message with the rule label, attribute name, and rule class ID, producing something like `\"//pkg:my_target: missing value for mandatory attribute 'out' in 'my_rule' rule\"`.\n\nNow I'm checking what happens with integer default values like `attr.int(default = 42)` by looking at how the attribute system handles them.\n\nWhen the Starlark call is parsed, the default value gets wrapped as `StarlarkInt.of(42)` and stored in the attribute descriptor. Later, when a rule retrieves this value through `RawAttributeMapper.get(\"num\", Type.INTEGER)`, it comes back as `StarlarkInt` since that's the internal representation for `Type.INTEGER`. handling.\n */\n@RunWith(JUnit4.class)\npublic final class StarlarkRuleEvalTest extends PackageLoadingTestCase {\n\n  @Before\n  public void setUpCustomRule() throws Exception {\n _impl,\n            attrs = {\n                \"srcs\": attr.label_list(allow_files = True),\n                \"out\": attr.string(mandatory = True),\n                \"deps\": attr.label_list(),\n                \"num\": attr.int(default = 42),\n            },\n        )\n        \"\"\");\n  }\n\n  private Package loadPackage(String pkgid) throws Exception {\n    return getTarget(\"//\" + pkgid + \":BUILD\").getPackage();\n  }\n\n  /** Test 1: Basic rule instantiation with required attributes. */\n  @Test\n  public void testBasicRuleInstantiation() throws Exception {\n    scratch.file(\n        \"pkg/BUILD\",\n        \"\"\"\n        load(\"//rules:my_rule.bzl\", \"my_rule\")\n\n        my_rule(\n            name = \"my_target\",\n            out = \"output.txt\",\n        )\n        \"\"\");\n\n    Rule rule = (Rule) getTarget(\"//pkg:my_target\");\n    assertThat(rule).isNotNull();\n    assertThat(rule.getName()).isEqualTo(\"my_target\");\n    assertThat(rule.getRuleClass()).isEqualTo(\"my_rule\");\n    assertThat(rule.containsErrors()).isFalse();\n  }\n\n  /** Test 2: Missing required attribute produces an error. */\n  @Test\n  public void testMissingRequiredAttributeProducesError() throws Exception {\n    reporter.removeHandler(failFastHandler);\n    scratch.file(\n        \"pkg/BUILD\",\n        \"\"\"\n        load(\"//rules:my_rule.bzl\", \"my_rule\")\n\n        my_rule(name = \"my_target\")\n        \"\"\");\n\n    Package pkg = loadPackage(\"pkg\");\n    assertThat(pkg.containsErrors()).isTrue();\n    assertContainsEvent(\"missing value for mandatory attribute 'out' in 'my_rule' rule\");\n  }\n\n  /** Test 3: Attribute type validation — passing a list where a string is expected. */\n  @Test\n  public void testAttributeTypeValidation() throws Exception {\n    reporter.removeHandler(failFastHandler);\n    scratch.file(\n        \"pkg/BUILD\",\n        \"\"\"\n        load(\"//rules:my_rule.bzl\", \"my_rule\")\n\n        my_rule(\n            name = \"my_target\",\n            out = [\"not_a_string\"],\n        )\n        \"\"\");\n\n    Package pkg = loadPackage(\"pkg\");\n    assertThat(pkg.containsErrors()).isTrue();\n    assertContainsEvent(\"expected value of type 'string'\");\n  }\n\n  /** Test 4: Default attribute values are applied when not specified. */\n  @Test\n  public void testDefaultAttributeValuesApplied() throws Exception {\n    scratch.file(\n        \"pkg/BUILD\",\n        \"\"\"\n        load(\"//rules:my_rule.bzl\", \"my_rule\")\n\n        my_rule(\n            name = \"my_target\",\n            out = \"output.txt\",\n        )\n        \"\"\");\n\n    Rule rule = (Rule) getTarget(\"//pkg:my_target\");\n    assertThat(rule.containsErrors()).isFalse();\n\n    AttributeMap attrs = RawAttributeMapper.of(rule);\n    assertThat(attrs.get(\"num\", Type.INTEGER)).isEqualTo(StarlarkInt.of(42));\n    assertThat(attrs.get(\"srcs\", BuildType.LABEL_LIST)).isEmpty();\n  }\n\n  /** Test 5: Visibility attribute can be set and is reflected in the rule. */\n  @Test\n  public void testVisibilityAttributeHandling() throws Exception {\n    scratch.file(\n        \"pkg/BUILD\",\n        \"\"\"\n        load(\"//rules:my_rule.bzl\", \"my_rule\")\n\n        my_rule(\n            name = \"my_public_target\",\n            out = \"output.txt\",\n            visibility = [\"//visibility:public\"],\n        )\n        \"\"\");\n\n    Rule rule = (Rule) getTarget(\"//pkg:my_public_target\");\n    assertThat(rule).isNotNull();\n    assertThat(rule.containsErrors()).isFalse();\n    assertThat(rule.getVisibility().getDeclaredLabels())\n        .containsExactly(RuleVisibility.PUBLIC_LABEL);\n  }\n\n  /** Test 6: A valid rule name is accepted without errors. */\n  @Test\n  public void testValidRuleName() throws Exception {\n    scratch.file(\n        \"pkg/BUILD\",\n        \"\"\"\n        load(\"//rules:my_rule.bzl\", \"my_rule\")\n\n        my_rule(\n            name = \"valid_rule_name\",\n            out = \"output.txt\",\n        )\n        \"\"\");\n\n    Rule rule = (Rule) getTarget(\"//pkg:valid_rule_name\");\n    assertThat(rule).isNotNull();\n    assertThat(rule.getName()).isEqualTo(\"valid_rule_name\");\n    assertThat(rule.containsErrors()).isFalse();\n  }\n\n  /** Test 7: An invalid rule name (containing ':') produces an error. */\n  @Test\n  public void testInvalidRuleName() throws Exception {\n    reporter.removeHandler(failFastHandler);\n    scratch.file(\n        \"pkg/BUILD\",\n        \"\"\"\n        load(\"//rules:my_rule.bzl\", \"my_rule\")\n\n        my_rule(name = \"invalid:name\", out = \"output.txt\")\n        \"\"\");\n\n    Package pkg = loadPackage(\"pkg\");\n    assertThat(pkg.containsErrors()).isTrue();\n    assertContainsEvent(\"target names may not contain ':'\");\n  }\n}\n","numLines":197,"startLine":1,"totalLines":197}}}
